metadata.title: Governance of artificial intelligence in Southeast Asia

abstract: Governance of artificial intelligence (AI) has not achieved global participation. The primary state-led instrument focusing specifically on the global governance of artificial intelligence is the Global Partnership on Artificial Intelligence (GPAI). Although GPAI aims for broad international participation, the only GPAI member from Southeast Asia is Singapore. GPAI's imbalanced global participation, restrictive membership process, and limited translations are potential barriers to Southeast Asian participation. However, a comparative policy analysis suggests that GPAI members and nonmembers in Southeast Asia have AI governance policies which are largely compatible, despite key differences. This study uses quantitative topic-modeling and qualitative content analysis to compare the AI governance policies of Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam with the policies of Australia and Singapore, as reference GPAI members. The policies of GPAI, Australia, and Singapore emphasise the function of ethics while the policies of Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam emphasise the function of human capital development. State-led, global AI governance efforts could attract more Southeast Asian participation by further emphasising human capital development and deemphasising the function of ethics. GPAI could increase the likelihood of Southeast Asian participation by decreasing its emphasis on political systems, allowing intergovernmental organisations to join, and recruiting all G20 members.

sections.paper_id: Keith - 2024 - Governance of artificial intelligence in Southeast Asia
paragraphs: Contemporary artificial intelligence (AI) systems are characterised primarily by domain-specific machine learning algorithms and large language models (Ulnicane et al., 2021). These systems have achieved high levels of performance in a broad range of tasks and are widely available (Anthropic, 2024;Vinyals et al., 2019). However, global governance for AI systems is just beginning to emerge. To achieve worldwide adherence to guidance and norms for the use of AI, efforts at global governance will need to attract more widespread participation (Butcher & Beridze, 2019;Taeihagh, 2021). Clarifying different approaches to national and global AI governance in Southeast Asia could improve global governance of AI by making global governance structures more relevant to key countries currently outside the global, state-led AI governance structure.

Southeast Asia is an important region for global governance of AI because of the region's economic dynamism, relatively high level of geopolitical tension, and active regional AI governance through the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN). The ASEAN Guide on AI Governance and Ethics (2024) establishes regional AI principles for Southeast Asia and provides a framework for policymakers. The ASEAN guide notes that governments and international organisations have issued similar principles and frameworks but argues that "there has not yet been an intergovernmental common standard for AI that defines the principles of AI governance" (ASEAN, 2024, p.7). Although the ASEAN guide is regionally focused, it specifically identifies the Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD) Recommendation of the Council on AI (2019), United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO) Recommendation on the Ethics of AI (2022), and European Union (EU) Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI (2019) as references which influenced its development. Additionally, the guide's objectives include helping organisations "align their AI practices with international standards and best practices" and the guide's regional recommendations include an ASEAN Working Group on AI Governance which can "lead efforts to further international cooperation on AI governance approaches, including through engaging ASEAN's various dialogue partners" (ASEAN, 2024).

This regional emphasis on international cooperation reinforces national strategies which emphasise international alignment in Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam. The international engagement is also reflected in their approval of the UNESCO recommendations. Despite the regional policy, national policies, and behaviors supporting international engagement on AI governance, Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam have not participated in the primary state-led governance organisation focusing specifically on AI, the Global Partnership on Artificial Intelligence (GPAI). Better understanding how the AI policies of Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam align or do not align with the existing state-led global governance structure illuminates how the global governance structure might accommodate any differences. This study's research aim is to critically compare national and state-led global governance of AI in Southeast Asia. The primary research question is as follows: what barriers and policy differences influence Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam's participation in state-led, global governance focusing on AI?
Research sub-questions include:

• What roles, functions, and policy instruments are emphasised in GPAI governance policy? • What roles, functions, and policy instruments are emphasised in the AI governance policies of Singapore and Australia (GPAI members), and of Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam (GPAI nonmembers)? • How does the approach to AI governance differ among GPAI, Singapore, Australia, Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam? This research finds that the primary policy difference is that the policies of GPAI, Australia, and Singapore emphasise the function of ethics while the policies of Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam emphasise the function of human capital. In addition to policy differences, GPAI's imbalanced global participation, restrictive membership process, and limited translations are potential barriers to Southeast Asian participation.

To develop policy recommendations addressing these barriers and policy differences, this article first introduces key stakeholders and reviews the global landscape of AI governance with a focus on Southeast Asian perspectives. Then, the article identifies potential barriers which might discourage Southeast Asian countries from joining GPAI. Next, the article introduces the quantitative and qualitative methods for analyzing the AI governance policies of a subset of GPAI members and nonmembers. After applying two complementary methods, the article presents the policy roles, functions, and instruments that each nation's policy emphasises and discusses differences and similarities in the AI governance policies. The article concludes with policy recommendations to address the potential barriers and policy differences identified in the discussion.

(2020) characterise international governance of AI as fragmented and they highlight GPAI, the OECD principles, and the G20 principles as major international AI policy initiatives in addition to private fora. While the United Nations (UN), OECD, and G20 participate in AI governance, their scope is general, unlike GPAI. Additional global AI governance organisations have been proposed but are not yet established, such as the draft Council of Europe legally binding international convention on AI and human rights (Ho et al., 2023;Roberts et al., 2024). Accordingly, this study focuses on GPAI as the primary state-led governance organisation focusing specifically on AI. GPAI is a multistakeholder initiative with 29 international partners aiming to "guide the responsible development and use of AI" (GPAI, 2024). The international partners include 28 countries and the European Union. Additionally, GPAI solicits experts from science, industry, civil society, trade unions, and international organisations to voluntarily contribute to its Multistakeholder Expert Group and Expert Working Groups in an individual capacity (GPAI, 2024). As Schmitt (2020) notes, although GPAI is a multistakeholder initiative, it is stateled and only state members control the GPAI Executive Council and Ministerial Council, which have authority over the GPAI structure and processes. In Dingwerth and Pattberg's (2022) organisation of global governance theories, GPAI is aligned with systems, statecentric, and formal theories. GPAI is one of the few intergovernmental AI governance fora that is not regionally focused, and it has more "depth" compared to other AI governance approaches in terms of stricter rules and more ambitious commitments (Cihon et al., 2020;Veale et al., 2023). Additionally, Png (2022) identifies GPAI as a high-level initiative with an inclusive multistakeholder approach, which has the potential to mitigate the "systematic disadvantages of exclusionary dynamics for the Global South" in contemporary global AI governance. GPAI's depth and inclusive aims make it an important global governance organisation for AI.

However, Singapore is the only Southeast Asian member of GPAI. If Southeast Asian countries do not participate as GPAI members at the national level, they are not able to vote on key global AI governance decisions or fill influential council roles for global AI policymaking. GPAI member voting privileges and chair positions would allow Southeast Asian states to influence the selection of expert working group themes, the establishment of Expert Support Centers, locations of global meetings, governance funding structures, recommendations on new members, and modifications to GPAI structures and processes (GPAI, 2020). The net result of not participating in GPAI is that Southeast Asia is less likely to benefit from international AI research and collaboration, AI expert working group outputs are less responsive to Southeast Asian priorities, and the logistical and monetary costs of participating in global AI governance are higher. In a broader sense, regionally determined participation in GPAI also negatively contributes to the fragmented AI governance landscape, which "further problematizes cooperation problems as it allows countries and companies to follow different policies and standards for AI" (Roberts et al., 2024).

The relevance of AI governance is not uniform across the Southeast Asian region (Dai, 2019). Several Southeast Asian countries have moderately high levels of AI readiness but have not joined GPAI, which specifically includes AI expertise as a membership criterion, evaluated partially through an AI readiness index. As there is no explicit minimum threshold of AI readiness for membership in GPAI, the index values of current GPAI members provide the best F I G U R E 1 East Asia Summit defense and economic architecture.

estimate of acceptable AI readiness level for prospective members. In Southeast Asia, only Singapore, Indonesia, Malaysia, and Thailand have AI readiness levels above the 15th percentile of GPAI members (Rogerson et al., 2022, p. 41). National policy on AI governance also varies among Southeast Asian states. Three databases were searched for AI policies related to national governance and ethical guidance: OECD AI Policy Observatory (2023), AI Governance Database (2023), and AI Ethics Guidelines Global Inventory (2023). Singapore, Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam each had AI policies published in at least one database. Although Vietnam has AI readiness in the 11th percentile of GPAI members, it has a mature body of AI-related policy. In contrast, other Southeast Asian states do not have published national AI strategies and have lower AI readiness levels. Based on the AI readiness level and maturity of national policies, this study focuses on Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam.

Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam have different relationships with the major economic and defense arrangements in the Asia-Pacific, shown in Figure 1. However, GPAI membership does not match the membership for any other existing structure. To better understand the four nonmembers under consideration, two GPAI members are evaluated for comparison. Singapore is selected for its potential similarity as the only GPAI member in the ASEAN. Australia is selected for its potential dissimilarity as the only GPAI member included in every major regional defense and economic structure except ASEAN.

Several Southeast Asian countries have moderately high levels of AI readiness but have not joined GPAI, which specifically includes AI expertise as a membership criterion, evaluated partially through an AI readiness index. As there is no explicit minimum threshold of AI readiness for membership in GPAI, the index values of current GPAI members provide the best F I G U R E 1 East Asia Summit defense and economic architecture.

estimate of acceptable AI readiness level for prospective members. In Southeast Asia, only Singapore, Indonesia, Malaysia, and Thailand have AI readiness levels above the 15th percentile of GPAI members (Rogerson et al., 2022, p. 41). National policy on AI governance also varies among Southeast Asian states. Three databases were searched for AI policies related to national governance and ethical guidance: OECD AI Policy Observatory (2023), AI Governance Database (2023), and AI Ethics Guidelines Global Inventory (2023). Singapore, Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam each had AI policies published in at least one database. Although Vietnam has AI readiness in the 11th percentile of GPAI members, it has a mature body of AI-related policy. In contrast, other Southeast Asian states do not have published national AI strategies and have lower AI readiness levels. Based on the AI readiness level and maturity of national policies, this study focuses on Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam.

Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam have different relationships with the major economic and defense arrangements in the Asia-Pacific, shown in Figure 1. However, GPAI membership does not match the membership for any other existing structure. To better understand the four nonmembers under consideration, two GPAI members are evaluated for comparison. Singapore is selected for its potential similarity as the only GPAI member in the ASEAN. Australia is selected for its potential dissimilarity as the only GPAI member included in every major regional defense and economic structure except ASEAN.

sections.paper_id: Keith - 2024 - Governance of artificial intelligence in Southeast Asia
paragraphs: Global and national perspectives on AI governance have been well-studied in China, the EU, and the United States (US). This literature reveals areas of convergence but also key differences, suggesting that perspectives on global governance of AI vary across regions. The State Council for the People's Republic of China published the National New Generation AI Plan (2017), addressing the same topics as the OECD AI principles, among other topics. In 2021, China released eight AI-related national policies, including the 14th Five-Year Plan for National Economic and Social Development of the People's Republic of China (2021), focused on research and development, and the Ethical Norms for New Generation AI (2021), again addressing the same topics as the OECD AI principles along with other topics. Cheng and Zeng (2022) explore China's approach to global AI governance and argue that China desires to lead global AI governance but has encountered difficulties with respect to the GPAI because of its liberal values. However, China has approved the G20 AI principles which have significant overlap with the GPAI values. Similarly, Roberts et al. (2022) compare AI governance aims and ethics in China and the EU and find that the aims are largely similar although the EU has been more proactive and formal in pursuing its ethical goals. On May 21, 2024, the European Council formally adopted the EU AI Act (2024). The EU's legal framework contrasts with China's current approach of policy guidance and the US's current approach of executive orders rather than more formal national policies for top-level AI governance strategy. Additionally, the US's Executive Order on the Safe, Secure, and Trustworthy Development and Use of Artificial Intelligence addresses only four of the five OECD AI principles (The White House, 2023). However, Stanley-Lockman (2022) compares the US perspective on responsible AI with those of its allies, finding broad convergence and support for AI governance with few areas of divergence.

In contrast, Southeast Asia has received less scholarly attention. Although Djeffal et al. ( 2022) consider Indonesia's AI governance strategy in a comparative international context, their focus is on quantifying the number of policy instruments in various categories rather than content analysis. There are also several recent papers which consider national governance of AI in Indonesia (Simanjuntak et al., 2024;Wadipalapa et al., 2024), Malaysia (Leong & Freeman, 2024;Manap & Abdullah, 2020), and Thailand (Hongladarom, 2021). However, in each case, the focus is at the national or sub-national level without systematic international comparisons or consideration of participation in global governance.

Research has also directly investigated reasons why AI governance approaches differ globally. Butcher and Beridze (2019, p. 95) argue that global governance of AI might achieve broader participation if it focuses on narrow areas such as health care or lethal autonomous weapon systems. Erman and Furendal (2024, p. 10) argue that global AI governance requires democratic participation to develop priorities from the political entities who are affected by its policies. Similarly, research has highlighted several critical concerns specific to the Global South, including the "paradox of participation" (relating to the international cooperation function in this study's framework) and workers' rights (relating to the human capital function in this study's framework) (Png, 2022;Sampath, 2021). Additionally, the global discourse on AI governance is skewed toward precautionary values rather than optimistic values, which Jecker and Nakazawa (2022) argue represents the disproportionate influence of North American and European influence compared to Japanese influence. To mitigate barriers to cross-cultural cooperation between North America, Europe, and East Asia, ÓhÉigeartaigh et al. (2020, p. 585) recommend translation of key AI reports and geographic variety in conference locations. Similarly, the regime fragmentation in AI governance policy imposes participation costs and stretches AI expertise resources disproportionately for developing countries (Cihon et al., 2020, p. 548).

At the level of national AI governance, Jobin et al. ( 2019) catalog national perspectives on AI ethics (including Singapore) and find global convergence on five ethical principles: transparency, justice and fairness, non-maleficence, responsibility, and privacy. Although this convergence suggests some degree of global alignment and shared reputational stakes, critics have highlighted the possibility of "ethics washing" as a form of strategic self-regulation to avoid more stringent regulatory intervention (Krum & Greenhill, 1972;van Maanen, 2022). Beyond ethics, Radu (2021) evaluates the national strategies of China, Japan, Singapore, South Korea, and eight countries outside the Indo-Pacific using a hybrid governance framework. The research finds broad use of mixed public, private, and other institutional logics in governance of AI with deliberately vague mandates.

In addition to formal policy perspectives, recent studies have explored public perceptions on AI governance. Ehret (2022) provides survey-based evidence for perspectives from India, China, and three countries outside of the Indo-Pacific. Kelley et al. (2021) provide survey-based evidence for perspectives from India, South Korea, Australia, and five countries outside the Indo-Pacific. Both studies find significant differences in perspectives from different national populations.

The existing literature on global AI governance has compared general AI governance policies across several countries, including India, China, South Korea, Australia, Singapore, and Indonesia in the Indo-Pacific. However, the reasons GPAI participation has not expanded to Southeast Asia have not been identified in the literature. This represents a population gap, in that AIready countries in Southeast Asia have not been compared in the international context, although Singapore is well-researched and Indonesia was included in one comparative analysis (Robinson et al., 2011). It also represents a practical-knowledge gap, in that the differences in Southeast Asian government practices with respect to global AI governance have not been explained by research (Miles, 2017). The consequence of these population and practical-knowledge gaps is that the understanding of AI governance functions and policy instruments is incomplete for a geographic region that is rapidly developing and geopolitically active. This gap negatively contributes to the historic and continuing lack of attention to Asia in the mainstream international relations literature (Acharya & Buzan, 2017;Johnston, 2012;Kang, 2003). It also fails to address the governance aspects that are unique to countries in Southeast Asia (Beeson, 2001).

sections.paper_id: Keith - 2024 - Governance of artificial intelligence in Southeast Asia
paragraphs: There are several potential barriers that might limit GPAI participation in Southeast Asia. First, disparities in foreign relations preferences between members and nonmembers might affect the perception of participation. The G20 has long had a legitimacy gap related to lack of regional representation, including in Southeast Asia (Cooper, 2011). Only five G20 members are not participants in GPAI: China, Indonesia, Russia, Saudi Arabia, and South Africa. Furthermore, the GPAI terms of reference explicitly restrict membership to countries and the EU, excluding ASEAN and other regional intergovernmental organisations (GPAI, 2020, p. 3). The OECD is also granted permanent observer status (GPAI, 2020, p. 5). The privileged position of Europeanfocused institutions in GPAI contrasts with Southeast Asian policy positions supporting ASEAN centrality (ASEAN, 2022). Regardless of the objectives and content of GPAI, Indonesia's participation in GPAI might be perceived as taking sides which could make its bebas aktif policy of nonalignment more difficult to implement. Although there is global participation in GPAI, including Brazil and India, the membership is disproportionately from high-income countries. GPAI could reduce this barrier by including ASEAN and other regional institutions as eligible members, recruiting the remaining G20 countries, recruiting non-European countries, and granting permanent observer status to other non-European organisations.

For Southeast Asia in particular, ASEAN membership in GPAI, rather than national membership, might be a more direct path to participation from each member country, given the emphasis on ASEAN centrality among members. Such a change is feasible considering that the GPAI Terms of Reference are reviewed at least every three years or at the discretion of the Executive Council (GPAI, 2020, p. 15). Notably, the GPAI Ministers' Declaration from the 4th Session of the GPAI Council included a comment that the GPAI Ministers "welcome the revision of GPAI's Terms of Reference" related to financial mechanisms (GPAI, 2022, p. 2). This precedent suggests that further revisions to the Terms of Reference aimed at attracting more participation would be feasible. Furthermore, the development of ASEAN's regional AI governance guide reflects the endorsement of international governance in Indonesia, Malaysia, and Vietnam's AI strategies, which explicitly reference global governance in the case of Indonesia and Malaysia.

Additionally, the internal and external membership process might limit participation from Southeast Asian countries. From the external national perspective, GPAI is a relatively young organisation, established in 2020. Notably, Singapore and Australia published national AI governance strategies prior to GPAI's establishment while Thailand, Indonesia, and Vietnam published their AI governance strategies afterwards. These timing differences suggest a possibility that nonmembers' lack of participation in GPAI could be a temporary situation reflecting the length of national bureaucratic decision processes or a sequencing of regional then global participation, rather than a specific intent not to join. However, Southeast Asian countries have already supported UNESCO global AI governance, published national policies which include explicit support for participation in international AI governance, and the GPAI's membership process has substantial internal barriers to Southeast Asian participation, suggesting that other explanations are more likely.

The GPAI membership process could act as a significant barrier. The GPAI Steering Committee subjectively assesses GPAI applicants based on a letter of intent produced by the applicant and a set of quantitative metrics. The Steering Committee assesses the applicant's commitment to the OECD AI principles, role in advancing responsible AI, and level of AI expertise without setting explicit numerical thresholds for the metrics (GPAI, 2021). GPAI published a "Frame for letter of intent and reference metrics to support the assessment of GPAI Membership" (2021), which includes suggested metrics and questions from the GPAI Steering Committee on Membership. Table 1 summarises the current value of those suggested metrics for each country under consideration and the quartiles of GPAI members' values (excluding Australia and Singapore). 1 Because there are no explicit thresholds for the metrics, the values of current GPAI members provide the best approximation of acceptable ranges for applicants.

The GPAI membership criteria cited in the framework do not refer to democracy, yet 3 of the 11 suggested metrics explicitly focus on democracy. Furthermore, the largest group of metrics (5 of 11) are related to general values and principles without any direct connection to AI. The GPAI mission aims to take "into particular account the interests, contributions, and priorities of emerging and developing countries" while bringing "together perspectives from countries and territories with differing contexts, backgrounds, and experience" (GPAI, 2020, p. 2). In contrast, the membership assessment framework makes no reference to differing contexts or the priorities of emerging and developing countries. Indonesia, Malaysia, and Vietnam have metric scores above the lowest GPAI member for all metrics related to technical expertise and responsible AI, other than the AI & Democratic Values index. However, Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam have lower scores, sometimes below the lowest GPAI member, for metrics related to democracy and freedom. GPAI could reduce the barriers to membership by selecting a different set of suggested metrics that more closely align with the GPAI mission and by including transparent threshold values for the metrics and responses. Lastly, GPAI material is published only in English and the closely related OECD AI Observatory website is published in English and French. Indonesia's AI governance strategy is published only in Bahasa Indonesia, Thailand's strategy is primarily published in Thai with a short English summary, and Vietnam's strategy is published in both English and Vietnamese. Including translations of key GPAI material in Southeast Asian languages would lower barriers to participation.

Furthermore, the development of ASEAN's regional AI governance guide reflects the endorsement of international governance in Indonesia, Malaysia, and Vietnam's AI strategies, which explicitly reference global governance in the case of Indonesia and Malaysia.

Additionally, the internal and external membership process might limit participation from Southeast Asian countries. From the external national perspective, GPAI is a relatively young organisation, established in 2020. Notably, Singapore and Australia published national AI governance strategies prior to GPAI's establishment while Thailand, Indonesia, and Vietnam published their AI governance strategies afterwards. These timing differences suggest a possibility that nonmembers' lack of participation in GPAI could be a temporary situation reflecting the length of national bureaucratic decision processes or a sequencing of regional then global participation, rather than a specific intent not to join. However, Southeast Asian countries have already supported UNESCO global AI governance, published national policies which include explicit support for participation in international AI governance, and the GPAI's membership process has substantial internal barriers to Southeast Asian participation, suggesting that other explanations are more likely.

The GPAI membership process could act as a significant barrier. The GPAI Steering Committee subjectively assesses GPAI applicants based on a letter of intent produced by the applicant and a set of quantitative metrics. The Steering Committee assesses the applicant's commitment to the OECD AI principles, role in advancing responsible AI, and level of AI expertise without setting explicit numerical thresholds for the metrics (GPAI, 2021). GPAI published a "Frame for letter of intent and reference metrics to support the assessment of GPAI Membership" (2021), which includes suggested metrics and questions from the GPAI Steering Committee on Membership. Table 1 summarises the current value of those suggested metrics for each country under consideration and the quartiles of GPAI members' values (excluding Australia and Singapore). 1 Because there are no explicit thresholds for the metrics, the values of current GPAI members provide the best approximation of acceptable ranges for applicants.

The GPAI membership criteria cited in the framework do not refer to democracy, yet 3 of the 11 suggested metrics explicitly focus on democracy. Furthermore, the largest group of metrics (5 of 11) are related to general values and principles without any direct connection to AI. The GPAI mission aims to take "into particular account the interests, contributions, and priorities of emerging and developing countries" while bringing "together perspectives from countries and territories with differing contexts, backgrounds, and experience" (GPAI, 2020, p. 2). In contrast, the membership assessment framework makes no reference to differing contexts or the priorities of emerging and developing countries. Indonesia, Malaysia, and Vietnam have metric scores above the lowest GPAI member for all metrics related to technical expertise and responsible AI, other than the AI & Democratic Values index. However, Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam have lower scores, sometimes below the lowest GPAI member, for metrics related to democracy and freedom. GPAI could reduce the barriers to membership by selecting a different set of suggested metrics that more closely align with the GPAI mission and by including transparent threshold values for the metrics and responses. Lastly, GPAI material is published only in English and the closely related OECD AI Observatory website is published in English and French. Indonesia's AI governance strategy is published only in Bahasa Indonesia, Thailand's strategy is primarily published in Thai with a short English summary, and Vietnam's strategy is published in both English and Vietnamese. Including translations of key GPAI material in Southeast Asian languages would lower barriers to participation.

paragraphs: To mitigate the subjectivity of qualitative content coding and the lack of nuance in quantitative topic modeling, this research employs a combined approach. The simultaneous, mixed-methods approach allows for more complete understanding of the documents (Heale & Forbes, 2013). Quantitative natural language processing (NLP) topic-modeling and qualitative content analysis are employed to investigate the core AI governance strategy documents listed in Table 2. This combined approach has been established by prior work in the AI governance literature and policy research literature (Isoaho et al., 2021;Papyshev & Yarime, 2023).

The quantitative topic-modeling method is a form of text classification. Previous work in this area has employed Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA), but this analysis uses zero-shot text classification. 2 The zero-shot approach improves upon the LDA approach by using a multi-label output that does not treat topic terms as mutually exclusive, since roles can coexist in sections of the documents. Additionally, the zero-shot approach allows direct classification of the roles of interest, rather than indirect manual assignment of roles to word groupings identified by LDA. Lastly, the zero-shot approach operates on small sections of text within the documents allowing for a granular estimation of role prevalence throughout the text of each document. In this application, the analysis applies zero-shot multi-label classification of the role terms (control, development, and promotion) to each policy in 500-character subsets (corresponding to approximately 1 to 3 sentences). It does not apply zero-shot classification to the functions or policy instruments because of the large number of categories.

The models used in the quantitative analysis are from Hugging Face (2023), a machine learning repository that hosts models which have been pre-trained on large sets of text. These models can be used to evaluate the degree to which subsets of text relate to certain concepts. Although models in each class perform the same basic function, the results of the text evaluation vary among models because of their different training texts and model architecture. The code and results are available at https://github. com/ ajkei th/ Strat egyDo cumen tAnal ysis/. 3 Deductive-dominant qualitative content analysis is used in combination with the quantitative analysis (Armat et al., 2018). This content analysis deductively identifies governance functions and policy instruments in the core documents using pre-existing categories from previous research findings by Papyshev and Yarime (2023). However, the analysis also inductively creates new categories when key segments of the texts do not fit the existing framework. Then, these functions and policy instruments are grouped into the theoretical typology of development, control, and promotion roles proposed in the literature on governance of sociotechnical systems (Borras & Edler, 2020;Ulnicane et al., 2021). In the context of AI governance, control includes functions related to ethics, regulation, data, and national security; development includes functions

TA B L E 2 AI Governance Policies. Organisation Policy Language GPAI Terms of Reference (2020) English Australia AI Technology Roadmap (Hajkowicz et al., 2019) English Indonesia Strategi Nasional Kecerdasan Artifisial Indonesia (Kelompok Kerja Penyusun Strategi Nasional untuk Kecerdasan Artifisial, 2020) Bahasa Indonesia Malaysia Industry 4WRD -National Policy on Industry 4.0 (Ministry of International Trade and Industry, 2018) English Singapore National AI Strategy (Smart Nation and Digital Government Office, 2019) English Thailand Thailand National AI Strategy and Action Plan Summary (AI Thailand, 2022) English Vietnam National Strategy on R&D and Application of AI (Socialist Republic of Viet Nam Government News, 2021)

English related to research, public sector applications, infrastructure, and national challenges; and promotion includes functions related to human capital, private sector support, diffusion, international cooperation, and financial support (Papyshev & Yarime, 2023, p. 15). This method relies only on content analysis of document-based data. Future work employing data from policymaker and expert interviews, surveys, or observations would complement this data to allow more detailed investigation of the motivations and plans related to AI governance in Southeast Asia.

paragraphs: The results from the zero-shot classification are displayed in Figure 2. The governance strategies of each country have a different emphasis on the roles of control, development, and promotion. The overall mean topic probabilities for Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam as a group are 0.30, 0.53, and 0.41 for control, development, and promotion, respectively. Compared to the GPAI topic probabilities of 0.54, 0.47, and 0.31, respectively, the non-GPAI countries emphasise development and promotion more and control less, on average. The non-GPAI countries emphasise control less than GPAI and Singapore; only Malaysia emphasises control more than Australia. Similarly, most non-GPAI countries emphasise development more than Australia; only Indonesia emphasises development less than GPAI, Australia, and Singapore. However, these differences are small in some cases and there is substantial variation in the distribution of role emphasis within the non-GPAI countries.

These results for Singapore and Australia can be compared to existing quantitative estimates of topic likelihood. For Australia's AI Action Plan, Papyshev and Yarime (2023, p. 15) find topic probabilities of approximately 0.25, 0.15, and 0.60 for control, development, and promotion, respectively, compared to 0.48, 0.48, and 0.34 using this analysis method. However, the Australian Department of Industry, Science and Resources (2023) lists Australia's AI Action Plan as an archived publication "published under the previous Australian Government." Additionally, at different times in 2023, the OECD AI Observatory has considered Australia's AI Action Plan or its AI Technology Roadmap to be the national AI strategy of Australia. For Australia's AI Technology Roadmap, the topic probabilities in this analysis are 0.42, 0.46, and 0.19, respectively. For Singapore, Papyshev and Yarime (2023, p. 15) find topic probabilities of approximately 0.2, 0.15, and 0.65 compared to 0.48, 0.50, and 0.32 in this analysis for control, development, and promotion, respectively. In both cases, there is substantial divergence between the prior results and these results. The differences are partially explained by the multi-label approach used here and by the general differences between LDA and zero-shot methods. The large differences highlight the continued importance of complementary qualitative document analysis.

For two of the countries of interest, the full governance strategy is only published in a non-English language. For Indonesia, classifying with English terms (control, development, promotion) results in estimated likelihoods of 0.23, 0.48, and 0.30, respectively. Classifying with Bahasa Indonesia terms (pengendalian, pengembangan, promosi) as a translation of (control, development, promotion) results in estimated likelihoods of 0.24, 0.41, and 0.13, respectively. The main difference is the likelihood associated with promotion and promosi. The summary analysis uses the Bahasa Indonesia topics to better match with the source text. For Thailand, there

is an official governance strategy in Thai and an official summary presentation in English. The sparse text in the presentation results in high variability and low sample size for zero-shot classification, so the full Thai text is used. Classifying the Thai text with English terms (control, development, promotion) results in estimated likelihoods of 0.16, 0.63, and 0.44, respectively. Classifying with Thai terms (การควบคุ ม, การพั ฒนา, การส่ งเสริ ม) as a translation of (control, development, promotion) results in estimated likelihoods of 0.12, 0.58, 0.46, respectively. The likelihood of the control and development topics are slightly lower using the Thai terms. The summary analysis also uses the Thai topics to better match with the source text.

F I G U R E 4 Distribution of AI governance roles (combined methods).

The top weighted role, top three qualitative functions, and top three qualitative policy instruments are summarised in Table 7. At the function level, GPAI, Australia, and Singapore all have ethics as a top three function but none of the non-GPAI countries give ethics the same emphasis. Conversely, all non-GPAI countries have human capital functions in their top three while no GPAI members do. While Singapore does not have human capital as a top three function, it does have multiple sub-national strategies for human capital development (Rigley et al., 2024). Although there is a difference in the degree to which GPAI and non-GPAI members emphasise ethics functions and human capital functions in their strategies, all countries under consideration address both functions to some degree. Ethics and human capital functions are not mutually exclusive.

For the AI policies considered in this study, Table 8 summarises the ethical principles referenced in each policy, based on the global ethical principles for AI categorised by Jobin et al. (2019). Indonesia stands out as comprehensively addressing all 11 ethical principles. Indonesia's policy also directly references its support for the G20 AI principles which are the same principles GPAI employs. The AI policies of Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam have varying coverage of ethical principles overall. However, none of these three policies address responsibility, freedom and autonomy, or dignity. Furthermore, Indonesia's discussion of freedom is framed as national freedom in the context of the national philosophy of Pancasila, rather than individual liberty (Kelompok Kerja Penyusun Strategi Nasional untuk Kecerdasan Artifisial, 2020, pp. 40-41). These three principles represent specific areas of potential difference despite the overall alignment of UNESCO and ASEAN ethical principles with the GPAI ethical principles.

In some cases, the qualitative analysis produces a similar distribution of roles compared to the quantitative analysis (Australia, Indonesia, Thailand). In other cases, there are substantial differences in the

order and magnitude of the roles (GPAI, Malaysia, Singapore, Vietnam). Notably, the qualitative coding of the Singapore document produces role proportions that are each within 5% of the previously published findings, suggesting some robustness in replication.

Overall, these findings clarify that there are policy differences related to specific functions and policy instruments, despite areas of overlap at the broad level of roles. This dynamic suggests that GPAI's policy priorities are not fully aligned with the functions and policy instruments prioritised by Southeast Asian countries.

The top weighted role, top three qualitative functions, and top three qualitative policy instruments are summarised in Table 7. At the function level, GPAI, Australia, and Singapore all have ethics as a top three function but none of the non-GPAI countries give ethics the same emphasis. Conversely, all non-GPAI countries have human capital functions in their top three while no GPAI members do. While Singapore does not have human capital as a top three function, it does have multiple sub-national strategies for human capital development (Rigley et al., 2024). Although there is a difference in the degree to which GPAI and non-GPAI members emphasise ethics functions and human capital functions in their strategies, all countries under consideration address both functions to some degree. Ethics and human capital functions are not mutually exclusive.

For the AI policies considered in this study, Table 8 summarises the ethical principles referenced in each policy, based on the global ethical principles for AI categorised by Jobin et al. (2019). Indonesia stands out as comprehensively addressing all 11 ethical principles. Indonesia's policy also directly references its support for the G20 AI principles which are the same principles GPAI employs. The AI policies of Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam have varying coverage of ethical principles overall. However, none of these three policies address responsibility, freedom and autonomy, or dignity. Furthermore, Indonesia's discussion of freedom is framed as national freedom in the context of the national philosophy of Pancasila, rather than individual liberty (Kelompok Kerja Penyusun Strategi Nasional untuk Kecerdasan Artifisial, 2020, pp. 40-41). These three principles represent specific areas of potential difference despite the overall alignment of UNESCO and ASEAN ethical principles with the GPAI ethical principles.

In some cases, the qualitative analysis produces a similar distribution of roles compared to the quantitative analysis (Australia, Indonesia, Thailand). In other cases, there are substantial differences in the

Overall, these findings clarify that there are policy differences related to specific functions and policy instruments, despite areas of overlap at the broad level of roles. This dynamic suggests that GPAI's policy priorities are not fully aligned with the functions and policy instruments prioritised by Southeast Asian countries.

sections.paper_id: Keith - 2024 - Governance of artificial intelligence in Southeast Asia
paragraphs: These findings suggest several policy implications with respect to ethics, policy content, and practical barriers. Despite the difference in emphasis on ethics, the content of ethical principles is widely shared from a global perspective, including Southeast Asia. GPAI, OECD, and the G20 have all endorsed the same set of AI ethical principles. Although democracy and human rights are sometimes considered areas of divergence between Europe and Asia (Kausikan, 1993, p. 36;Cheng & Zeng, 2022, p. 15), it is notable that the G20 endorsed the OECD AI Principles which contain references to both democracy and human rights (G20, 2019;OECD, 2019). Similarly, the ASEAN Charter includes explicit purposes "to strengthen democracy" and "to promote and protect human rights" (ASEAN, 2008, p. 4). Additionally, 193 members of UNESCO, including Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam, adopted the Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence (2022) which includes AI principles promoting human rights and democratic governance.

Although there is substantial overlap in the ethical guidelines and principles, recent research has highlighted the weak influence of ethical guidelines on applied behavior of AI professionals, particularly in settings without third-party ethical audits (Hagendorff, 2020, p. 108;Morley et al., 2021, p. 249). Nevertheless, the overlap in ethical guidelines suggests that the content of GPAI's references to ethics, democracy, and human rights are not a major barrier to participation from Southeast Asian countries. Rather, the primary difference is the extent to which those ethical principles are emphasised compared to other topics. This distinction suggests that the ethical content of global policy on AI is generally aligned with the ethical content of Southeast Asian policies on AI, but the function of ethics is overemphasised compared to other regional priorities.

In contrast, global policy on AI could increase acceptance in Southeast Asia by emphasising human capital promotion to align with priorities in Southeast Asian policies. Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam all emphasise human capital development functions in their top three functions while GPAI, Australia, and Singapore do not. Furthermore, Indonesia, Thailand, and Vietnam emphasise the role of development more than control or promotion. Better aligning the content of future GPAI strategies, policies, or working areas with roles and functions emphasised by Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam might attract greater participation.

Additionally, several nonmembers' strategies explicitly endorse high levels of participation or alignment with international governance. Vietnam's national AI governance strategy assigns the Ministry of Science and Technology to "join international AI associations, organisations and networks" (Socialist Republic of Viet Nam Government News, 2021, p. 6). Malaysia's strategy includes an action plan to "consolidate, harmonise, and align with global standards" (Ministry of International Trade and Industry, 2018, p. 61). Indonesia's strategy highlights cooperation with regional and global institutions as a strategic opportunity (Kelompok Kerja Penyusun Strategi Nasional untuk Kecerdasan Artifisial, 2020, p. 24). These references suggest a willingness to participate in global AI governance in a

TA B L E 8 Ethical principles in AI policies. Ethical principle GPAI AUS SGP IDN MYS THA VNM Transparency x x x x x x Justice and fairness x x x x x x x Non-maleficence x x x x x x x Responsibility x x x x Privacy x x x x x x Beneficence x x x x x x x Freedom and autonomy x x x Trust x x x x x Sustainability x x x x x x x Dignity x x Solidarity x general sense, although they are not specific to GPAI. GPAI might attract more Southeast Asian participation by engaging with the specific offices tasked to cooperate with international organisations identified by Vietnam and Indonesia. Conversely, from the perspective of policymakers in each individual country, policy changes related to open data could make membership in GPAI more likely. Like other Southeast Asian countries, Singapore has relatively low scores on GPAI's valuebased membership metrics, but it has relatively high scores for Government AI Readiness and Open Data.

Although it would be challenging for Southeast Asian countries to achieve Singapore's overall level of technological development, the specific area of Open Data is more promising in the short term. Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam should prioritise open access to government data related to budgets, geography, and the environment. Open data policies align with Indonesia, Malaysia, and Vietnam's explicit references to Open Data concepts in their strategies (Table 3) and with all four countries' emphasis on data centers, platforms, and marketplaces in their digital infrastructure (Table 4). Improved access to open data would increase their Open Data metric scores and the Data and Infrastructure Pillar of the Government AI Readiness metric. This policy change aligns with the countries' current AI strategies and would make their GPAI application profile more like Singapore's successful application profile.

In addition to national policy differences, this research suggests several other policy implications for the GPAI organisation which could address practical barriers to participating in AI governance. The GPAI Executive Council could increase the likelihood of Southeast Asian participation by amending its membership process to focus on AI rather than national political systems, while maintaining the content of its current ethical principles. Similarly, GPAI might be more attractive if it amended its Terms of Reference to allow ASEAN and other intergovernmental organisations to join as members. Lastly, Southeast Asian participation in GPAI may be more likely if GPAI recruits more ideologically diverse states and all the G20 members.

sections.paper_id: Keith - 2024 - Governance of artificial intelligence in Southeast Asia
paragraphs: The individual AI governance strategies of GPAI, Australia, Singapore, Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam differ substantially, especially at the level of policy instruments, but there are few systematic differences between GPAI members and nonmembers. The most salient policy differences for Southeast Asia are GPAI members' greater emphasis on the function of ethics compared to nonmembers, and nonmembers' greater emphasis on the function of human capital.

Additionally, GPAI has practical barriers to increased participation from Southeast Asian countries, including imbalanced global participation, restrictive membership processes, and limited translations.

These barriers and policy differences influence Southeast Asia's relative lack of participation in stateled, global AI governance through GPAI. However, open questions remain with respect to Southeast Asian states' motivations for participation in global AI governance. Interviews with GPAI policymakers and Southeast Asian national policymakers and experts could provide important detail and context on the status of any planned or in-progress applications to GPAI and other non-state or general global governance initiatives. From a broader perspective, organisational behavior or institutional frameworks might also provide more insight into state participation in global AI governance. Enhanced understanding of differences in governance approaches would lead to a stronger global governance structure for AI.
